questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Calculate the cutoff wavelength of the continuous X-rays produced by an X-ray tube operating at $30 \text{ kV}$.
questions.solution:
The cutoff wavelength of continuous X-rays is given by the Duane-Hunt law:

$\lambda_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$

Substituting the standard value $hc \approx 1242 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}$ and $V = 30 \text{ kV} = 30000 \text{ V}$:

$\lambda_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{1242 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}}{30000 \text{ eV}}$

$\lambda_{\text{min}} = 0.0414 \text{ nm}$

Since $1 \text{ nm} = 1000 \text{ pm}$:

$\lambda_{\text{min}} = 41.4 \text{ pm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the energy, the frequency, and the momentum of an X-ray photon having a wavelength of $0.10 \text{ nm}$.
questions.solution:
Given wavelength $\lambda = 0.10 \text{ nm} = 10^{-10} \text{ m}$.

The energy of the photon is given by $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$.

Using $hc \approx 1240 \text{ eV nm}$,

$E = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV nm}}{0.10 \text{ nm}} = 12400 \text{ eV} = 12.4 \text{ keV}$.

The frequency of the photon is given by $\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$.

$\nu = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}}{10^{-10} \text{ m}} = 3 \times 10^{18} \text{ Hz}$.

The momentum of the photon is given by $p = \dfrac{h}{\lambda}$.

$p = \dfrac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}}{10^{-10} \text{ m}} = 6.62 \times 10^{-24} \text{ kg m s}^{-1}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Iron emits a $K_\alpha$ X-ray with an energy of $6.4 \text{ keV}$, while calcium emits a $K_\alpha$ X-ray with an energy of $3.69 \text{ keV}$. Determine the time taken by an iron $K_\alpha$ photon and a calcium $K_\alpha$ photon to traverse a distance of $3 \text{ km}$.
options: $10 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$ by the iron photon and $17.3 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$ by the calcium photon
$17.3 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$ by the iron photon and $10 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$ by the calcium photon
questions.solution:
Both iron $K_\alpha$ and calcium $K_\alpha$ are X-ray photons, which are electromagnetic waves.

All electromagnetic waves travel at the speed of light in vacuum or air, which is $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$, irrespective of their energy or wavelength.

The distance to be traversed is $d = 3 \text{ km} = 3 \times 10^3 \text{ m}$.

The time taken by both photons is given by $t = \dfrac{d}{c}$.

$t = \dfrac{3 \times 10^3}{3 \times 10^8} = 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 10 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$.

Thus, both photons take $10 \text{ }\mu\text{s}$ to traverse the distance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: X-rays originating from a Coolidge tube have a cutoff wavelength of $80 \text{ pm}$. Determine the kinetic energy of the electrons striking the target.
questions.solution:
The cutoff wavelength $\lambda_{\text{min}}$ of X-rays is related to the maximum kinetic energy $K$ of the striking electrons by the equation:

$K = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{\text{min}}}$

Given $\lambda_{\text{min}} = 80 \text{ pm} = 0.08 \text{ nm}$.

Using the standard value $hc \approx 1240 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}$, we get:

$K = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}}{0.08 \text{ nm}}$

$K = 15500 \text{ eV} = 15.5 \text{ keV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What potential difference must be applied across an X-ray tube to generate X-rays with a wavelength of no less than $0.10 \text{ nm}$? Additionally, what is the maximum energy of a photon of this X-ray in joules?
questions.solution:
The minimum wavelength of X-rays produced in an X-ray tube is given by $\lambda_{min} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$.

Rearranging for the potential difference $V$:

$V = \dfrac{hc}{e\lambda_{min}}$

Using the value of $hc \approx 1240 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}$:

$V = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}}{e \times 0.10 \text{ nm}} = 12400 \text{ V} = 12.4 \text{ kV}$

The maximum energy of an X-ray photon corresponds to the minimum wavelength:

$E_{max} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{min}}$

Substituting the standard values in SI units:

$E_{max} = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{0.10 \times 10^{-9}}$

$E_{max} = 19.89 \times 10^{-16} \text{ J} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-15} \text{ J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an X-ray tube, the distance separating the cathode (filament) and the target is $1.5 \text{ m}$. Determine the electric field between the cathode and the target if the cutoff wavelength is given as $30 \text{ pm}$.
questions.solution:
The cutoff wavelength $\lambda_{\text{min}}$ is related to the accelerating voltage $V$ by the equation:

$\lambda_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$

Substituting the values $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$, $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$, and $\lambda_{\text{min}} = 30 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}$:

$V = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 30 \times 10^{-12}}$

$V = 41.4 \times 10^3 \text{ V} = 41.4 \text{ kV}$

The electric field $E$ between the cathode and the target is given by:

$E = \dfrac{V}{d}$

Substituting $V = 41.4 \text{ kV}$ and $d = 1.5 \text{ m}$:

$E = \dfrac{41.4}{1.5} = 27.6 \text{ kV/m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a colour TV, the electron beam is accelerated through $32 \text{ kV}$ before striking the screen. Determine the wavelength of the most energetic X-ray photon produced.
questions.solution:
The maximum energy of the X-ray photon produced is equal to the kinetic energy of the accelerated electron.

$E_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{\text{min}}} = eV$

$\lambda_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$

Substituting $hc \approx 1240 \text{ eV nm}$ and $V = 32000 \text{ V}$:

$\lambda_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{1240}{32000} \text{ nm}$

$\lambda_{\text{min}} = 0.03875 \text{ nm}$

Converting to picometers ($1 \text{ nm} = 1000 \text{ pm}$):

$\lambda_{\text{min}} = 0.03875 \times 1000 \text{ pm} = 38.75 \text{ pm} \approx 38.8 \text{ pm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the operating potential in an X-ray tube is raised by $1\%$, by what percentage will the cutoff wavelength decrease?
questions.solution:
The cutoff wavelength of X-rays produced in an X-ray tube is given by the relation:

$\lambda_{c} = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$

Taking the natural logarithm on both sides, we get:

$\ln \lambda_{c} = \ln \left(\dfrac{hc}{e}\right) - \ln V$

Differentiating the expression for small changes, we obtain:

$\dfrac{\Delta \lambda_{c}}{\lambda_{c}} = - \dfrac{\Delta V}{V}$

Multiplying by $100$ to get the percentage change:

$\dfrac{\Delta \lambda_{c}}{\lambda_{c}} \times 100 = - \left(\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} \times 100\right)$

Given that the operating potential is raised by $1\%$, we have $\dfrac{\Delta V}{V} \times 100 = 1\%$.

$\dfrac{\Delta \lambda_{c}}{\lambda_{c}} \times 100 = -1\%$

The negative sign indicates a decrease. Therefore, the cutoff wavelength decreases by $1\%$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When $40 \text{ kV}$ is applied across an X-ray tube, X-rays are obtained with a maximum frequency of $9.7 \times 10^{18} \text{ Hz}$. Determine the value of the Planck constant from these data.
questions.solution:
The maximum energy of an X-ray photon is equal to the kinetic energy of the incident electron.

$E_{\text{max}} = eV$

Given $V = 40 \text{ kV}$, the energy in electron-volts is:

$E_{\text{max}} = 40 \times 10^3 \text{ eV}$

Using the relation $E_{\text{max}} = h \nu_{\text{max}}$, the Planck constant $h$ is given by:

$h = \dfrac{E_{\text{max}}}{\nu_{\text{max}}}$

Substituting the given values:

$h = \dfrac{40 \times 10^3}{9.7 \times 10^{18}} \text{ eV s}$

$h \approx 4.12 \times 10^{-15} \text{ eV s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An X-ray tube operates at $40 \text{ kV}$. Assume that during each collision, the electron converts $70\%$ of its energy into a photon. Determine the lowest three wavelengths emitted from the tube. Disregard the energy imparted to the atom with which the electron collides.
questions.solution:
Initial energy of the electron, $E_0 = 40 \text{ keV}$.

In the first collision, the electron converts $70\%$ of its energy into a photon. The energy of the first photon is:
$E_1 = 0.70 \times 40 \text{ keV} = 28 \text{ keV}$

The remaining kinetic energy of the electron is:
$E_1' = 40 \text{ keV} - 28 \text{ keV} = 12 \text{ keV}$

In the second collision, the electron again converts $70\%$ of its remaining energy into a photon. The energy of the second photon is:
$E_2 = 0.70 \times 12 \text{ keV} = 8.4 \text{ keV}$

The remaining kinetic energy of the electron is:
$E_2' = 12 \text{ keV} - 8.4 \text{ keV} = 3.6 \text{ keV}$

In the third collision, the energy of the third photon is:
$E_3 = 0.70 \times 3.6 \text{ keV} = 2.52 \text{ keV}$

The corresponding wavelengths are given by $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E}$. Using $hc \approx 1242 \text{ keV}\cdot\text{pm}$:

$\lambda_1 = \dfrac{1242}{28} \approx 44.3 \text{ pm}$

$\lambda_2 = \dfrac{1242}{8.4} \approx 148 \text{ pm}$

$\lambda_3 = \dfrac{1242}{2.52} \approx 493 \text{ pm}$

These represent the lowest three wavelengths emitted from the tube.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For tungsten, the wavelength of the $K_\alpha$ X-ray is $21.3 \text{ pm}$. Ejecting an electron from the $L$ shell of a tungsten atom requires $11.3 \text{ keV}$. Determine the minimum accelerating voltage across an X-ray tube equipped with a tungsten target that will permit the emission of $K_\alpha$ X-rays.
questions.solution:
The energy of the emitted $K_\alpha$ X-ray photon is given by:

$E_{K_\alpha} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$

Using $hc \approx 1240 \text{ keV pm}$ and $\lambda = 21.3 \text{ pm}$:

$E_{K_\alpha} = \dfrac{1240 \text{ keV pm}}{21.3 \text{ pm}} \approx 58.2 \text{ keV}$

The $K_\alpha$ X-ray is emitted when an electron transitions from the L shell to a vacancy in the K shell. Therefore, the energy difference between the K and L shells is:

$E_{K_\alpha} = E_K - E_L$

Given the binding energy of the L shell $E_L = 11.3 \text{ keV}$, the binding energy of the K shell is:

$E_K = E_{K_\alpha} + E_L = 58.2 \text{ keV} + 11.3 \text{ keV} = 69.5 \text{ keV}$

To permit the emission of $K_\alpha$ X-rays, the incident electrons from the cathode must have sufficient kinetic energy to knock out an electron from the K shell of the tungsten target. Thus, the minimum kinetic energy required is $69.5 \text{ keV}$.

The minimum accelerating voltage $V$ across the X-ray tube is:

$V = \dfrac{E_K}{e} = 69.5 \text{ kV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An argon atom has a $K_\beta$ X-ray wavelength of $0.36 \text{ nm}$. To ionize an argon atom, a minimum energy of $16 \text{ eV}$ is required. Determine the energy necessary to knock out an electron from the $K$ shell of an argon atom.
questions.solution:
The $K_\beta$ X-ray is emitted when an electron transitions from the M shell to the K shell. The energy of the emitted $K_\beta$ photon is given by:

$E_{K_\beta} = E_M - E_K$

where $E_M$ and $E_K$ are the energies of the M and K shells, respectively.

The energy of the $K_\beta$ photon can be calculated using the given wavelength $\lambda = 0.36 \text{ nm}$:

$E_{K_\beta} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1242 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}}{0.36 \text{ nm}} = 3450 \text{ eV} = 3.45 \text{ keV}$

For an argon atom, the outermost electrons are in the M shell ($n=3$). The ionization energy is the energy required to remove an electron from this outermost shell to infinity. Therefore, the binding energy of the M shell is $16 \text{ eV}$, which means:

$E_M = -16 \text{ eV} = -0.016 \text{ keV}$

The energy required to knock out an electron from the K shell is the magnitude of the binding energy of the K shell, which is $-E_K$. Rearranging the transition energy equation:

$-E_K = E_{K_\beta} - E_M$

Substituting the values:

$-E_K = 3.45 \text{ keV} - (-0.016 \text{ keV}) = 3.45 \text{ keV} + 0.016 \text{ keV} = 3.466 \text{ keV}$

Rounding to two decimal places, the required energy is $3.47 \text{ keV}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Aluminium ($Z = 13$) and zinc ($Z = 30$) emit $K_\alpha$ X-rays with wavelengths of $887 \text{ pm}$ and $146 \text{ pm}$, respectively. Applying Moseley's law $\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z - b)$, determine the wavelength of the $K_\alpha$ X-ray emitted by iron ($Z = 26$).
questions.solution:
According to Moseley's law, the frequency $ u$ of the emitted X-ray is given by:
$\sqrt{ u} = a(Z - b)$

Since $ u = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$, we can write this in terms of wavelength $\lambda$ as:
$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}} = k(Z - b)$
where $k = \dfrac{a}{\sqrt{c}}$. This shows that $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda}}$ is a linear function of the atomic number $Z$.

Given the data for Aluminium ($Z = 13$) and Zinc ($Z = 30$):
$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{887}} = k(13 - b)$ ... (i)
$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{146}} = k(30 - b)$ ... (ii)

For Iron ($Z = 26$), let the wavelength be $\lambda_{Fe}$:
$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_{Fe}}} = k(26 - b)$ ... (iii)

Subtracting equation (i) from (ii) gives:
$k(30 - 13) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{146}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{887}}$
$17k \approx 0.08276 - 0.03358 = 0.04918$
$k \approx 0.002893 ext{ pm}^{-1/2}$

Now, we can find the value for Iron by subtracting $4k$ from the Zinc equation:
$k(26 - b) = k(30 - b) - 4k$
$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_{Fe}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{146}} - 4k$
$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_{Fe}}} \approx 0.08276 - 4(0.002893)$
$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_{Fe}}} \approx 0.08276 - 0.01157 = 0.07119 ext{ pm}^{-1/2}$

Squaring both sides to find $\lambda_{Fe}$:
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_{Fe}} \approx (0.07119)^2 \approx 0.005068$
$\lambda_{Fe} = \dfrac{1}{0.005068} \approx 197.3 ext{ pm}$

The closest value among the given options is $198 ext{ pm}$.

Answer: $198 ext{ pm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The $K_\alpha$ X-rays of aluminium ($Z = 13$) and zinc ($Z = 30$) possess wavelengths of $887 \text{ pm}$ and $146 \text{ pm}$, respectively. A particular element emits a $K_\alpha$ X-ray with an energy of $3.69 \text{ keV}$. Using Moseley's law $\sqrt{\nu} = a(Z - b)$, identify this element.
questions.solution:
According to Moseley's law for $K_\alpha$ X-rays, the frequency $ u$ is given by $\sqrt{ u} = a(Z - b)$.

For $K_\alpha$ lines, the screening constant $b \approx 1$. The energy of the emitted X-ray is given by:

$E = h u = Rhc(Z-1)^2 \left(\dfrac{1}{1^2} - \dfrac{1}{2^2} ight)$

Substituting $Rhc \approx 13.6 ext{ eV}$:

$E \approx 13.6 imes \dfrac{3}{4} (Z-1)^2 ext{ eV} = 10.2 (Z-1)^2 ext{ eV}$

Given the energy of the unknown element's $K_\alpha$ X-ray is $E = 3.69 ext{ keV} = 3690 ext{ eV}$:

$3690 = 10.2 (Z-1)^2$

$(Z-1)^2 = \dfrac{3690}{10.2} \approx 361.76$

$Z - 1 \approx \sqrt{361.76} \approx 19.02$

$Z \approx 20$

The atomic number $Z = 20$ corresponds to Calcium.

(Note: Using the given wavelengths for Al and Zn to find the exact empirical values of $a$ and $b$ also yields $Z \approx 20.2$, confirming the element is Calcium).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: By applying Moseley's law with $b = 1$, calculate the frequency of the $K_\alpha$ X-ray of $\text{La}$ ($Z = 57$) given that the frequency of the $K_\alpha$ X-ray of $\text{Cu}$ ($Z = 29$) is $1.88 \times 10^{18} \text{ Hz}$.
questions.solution:
According to Moseley's law, the frequency $ u$ of $K_\alpha$ X-ray is given by:

$\sqrt{ u} = a(Z - b)$

Given $b = 1$, we have $ u \propto (Z - 1)^2$.

For Cu ($Z = 29$):

$ u_{ ext{Cu}} \propto (29 - 1)^2 = 28^2$

For La ($Z = 57$):

$ u_{ ext{La}} \propto (57 - 1)^2 = 56^2$

Taking the ratio of the frequencies:

$\dfrac{ u_{ ext{La}}}{ u_{ ext{Cu}}} = \left(\dfrac{56}{28} ight)^2 = 2^2 = 4$

$ u_{ ext{La}} = 4 imes u_{ ext{Cu}}$

Substituting the given value $ u_{ ext{Cu}} = 1.88 imes 10^{18} ext{ Hz}$:

$ u_{ ext{La}} = 4 imes 1.88 imes 10^{18} ext{ Hz} = 7.52 imes 10^{18} ext{ Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The $K_\beta$ X-rays from certain elements are provided below.
ElementNePCaMnZnBr
Energy (keV)$0.858$$2.14$$4.02$$6.51$$9.57$$13.3$

If a Moseley-type plot of $\sqrt{\nu}$ versus $Z$ is drawn for the $K_\beta$ radiation, which of the following best describes the expected shape of the graph?
options: A straight line with a positive $Z$-intercept
A straight line with a negative $Z$-intercept
A parabola passing through the origin
A straight line passing through the origin

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The wavelengths of the $K_\alpha$ and $K_\beta$ X-rays of molybdenum are $0.71 \text{ \AA}$ and $0.63 \text{ \AA}$, respectively. Determine the wavelength of the $L_\alpha$ X-ray of molybdenum.
questions.solution:
The energy of an X-ray photon is given by $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$.

The $K_{\alpha}$ transition corresponds to an electron jumping from the $L$ shell to the $K$ shell, so $E_{K_{\alpha}} = E_L - E_K$.

The $K_{\beta}$ transition corresponds to an electron jumping from the $M$ shell to the $K$ shell, so $E_{K_{\beta}} = E_M - E_K$.

The $L_{\alpha}$ transition corresponds to an electron jumping from the $M$ shell to the $L$ shell, so $E_{L_{\alpha}} = E_M - E_L$.

From these energy differences, we can write:

$E_{L_{\alpha}} = E_{K_{\beta}} - E_{K_{\alpha}}$

Substituting $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$:

$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{L_{\alpha}}} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{K_{\beta}}} - \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{K_{\alpha}}}$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_{L_{\alpha}}} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{K_{\beta}}} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{K_{\alpha}}}$

$\lambda_{L_{\alpha}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{K_{\alpha}} \lambda_{K_{\beta}}}{\lambda_{K_{\alpha}} - \lambda_{K_{\beta}}}$

Given $\lambda_{K_{\alpha}} = 0.71 \text{ \AA}$ and $\lambda_{K_{\beta}} = 0.63 \text{ \AA}$:

$\lambda_{L_{\alpha}} = \dfrac{0.71 \times 0.63}{0.71 - 0.63}$

$\lambda_{L_{\alpha}} = \dfrac{0.4473}{0.08} = 5.59125 \text{ \AA}$

Rounding to two decimal places, we get $5.59 \text{ \AA}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a certain material, the wavelengths of the $K_\alpha$ and $L_\alpha$ X-rays are given as $21.3 \text{ pm}$ and $141 \text{ pm}$, respectively. Determine the wavelength of the $K_\beta$ X-ray for this material.
questions.solution:
The energy of an X-ray photon is related to its wavelength by $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$.

The transitions corresponding to the given X-rays are:
$K_\alpha$ transition: $L$ shell to $K$ shell ($E_{K\alpha} = E_L - E_K$)
$L_\alpha$ transition: $M$ shell to $L$ shell ($E_{L\alpha} = E_M - E_L$)
$K_\beta$ transition: $M$ shell to $K$ shell ($E_{K\beta} = E_M - E_K$)

From the energy levels:
$E_{K\beta} = E_{K\alpha} + E_{L\alpha}$

Substituting the energy-wavelength relation:
$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{K\beta}} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{K\alpha}} + \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{L\alpha}}$

$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_{K\beta}} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{K\alpha}} + \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{L\alpha}}$

Given $\lambda_{K\alpha} = 21.3 \text{ pm}$ and $\lambda_{L\alpha} = 141 \text{ pm}$:
$\dfrac{1}{\lambda_{K\beta}} = \dfrac{1}{21.3} + \dfrac{1}{141}$

$\lambda_{K\beta} = \dfrac{21.3 \times 141}{21.3 + 141}$

$\lambda_{K\beta} = \dfrac{3003.3}{162.3} \approx 18.5 \text{ pm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the operating voltage of an X-ray tube is raised to $1.5$ times its initial value, the short-wavelength limit undergoes a shift of $26 \text{ pm}$. Determine the initial operating voltage.
questions.solution:
The short-wavelength limit of an X-ray tube is given by $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$.

Let the initial operating voltage be $V$. The initial short-wavelength limit is $\lambda_1 = \dfrac{hc}{eV}$.

When the voltage is increased to $1.5 V$, the new short-wavelength limit becomes $\lambda_2 = \dfrac{hc}{1.5 eV} = \dfrac{2}{3} \lambda_1$.

The shift in the short-wavelength limit is given as $26 \text{ pm}$. Thus,

$\lambda_1 - \lambda_2 = 26 \text{ pm}$

$\lambda_1 - \dfrac{2}{3} \lambda_1 = 26 \text{ pm}$

$\dfrac{1}{3} \lambda_1 = 26 \text{ pm} \Rightarrow \lambda_1 = 78 \text{ pm}$

Using the value of $hc \approx 1240 \text{ eV nm} = 1240 \times 10^3 \text{ eV pm}$, the initial voltage $V$ is:

$V = \dfrac{hc}{e \lambda_1} = \dfrac{1240 \times 10^3 \text{ eV pm}}{e \times 78 \text{ pm}}$

$V = \dfrac{1240000}{78} \text{ V} \approx 15897 \text{ V} \approx 15.9 \text{ kV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Determine the maximum potential difference that may be applied across an X-ray tube with a tungsten target without emitting any characteristic $K$ or $L$ X-rays. The energy levels of the tungsten atom with an ejected electron are as follows.
Cell containing vacancy$K$$L$$M$
Energy in keV$69.5$$11.3$$2.3$
questions.solution:
For characteristic X-rays to be emitted, the incident electrons must possess sufficient kinetic energy to knock out an electron from the respective inner shell of the target atom.

From the given data, the energy required to create a vacancy in the $K$-shell (binding energy of the $K$-shell) is $69.5 \text{ keV}$, and the energy required to create a vacancy in the $L$-shell is $11.3 \text{ keV}$.

To avoid emitting any characteristic $K$ X-rays, the energy of the incident electrons must be less than $69.5 \text{ keV}$.
To avoid emitting any characteristic $L$ X-rays, the energy of the incident electrons must be less than $11.3 \text{ keV}$.

In order to emit neither $K$ nor $L$ characteristic X-rays, the maximum kinetic energy of the incident electrons must not exceed the binding energy of the $L$-shell.

Thus, the maximum kinetic energy is $E_{\text{max}} = 11.3 \text{ keV}$.

Since the kinetic energy of an electron accelerated through a potential difference $V$ is given by $E = eV$, the maximum potential difference that can be applied is:
$V = \dfrac{11.3 \text{ keV}}{e} = 11.3 \text{ kV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Operating at $40 \text{ kV}$, an X-ray tube has an electric current of $10 \text{ mA}$ flowing from the target to the filament. Assuming that, on average, $1\%$ of the total kinetic energy of the electrons striking the target is converted into X-rays, calculate the amount of heat produced in the target every second.
questions.solution:
The total electrical power supplied to the X-ray tube is given by $P = VI$.

Substituting the given values:

$P = 40 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} = 400 \text{ W} = 400 \text{ J/s}$

This represents the total kinetic energy of the electrons striking the target per second.

Since $1\%$ of the total kinetic energy is converted into X-rays, the remaining $99\%$ is converted into heat.

Heat produced per second $= \dfrac{99}{100} \times 400 = 396 \text{ J}$

Thus, the amount of heat produced in the target every second is $396 \text{ J}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An X-ray tube operating at $20 \text{ kV}$ produces heat at a rate of $200 \text{ W}$. Determine the current in the circuit, assuming that only a small fraction of the kinetic energy of the electrons is converted into X-rays.
questions.solution:
Since only a small fraction of the kinetic energy of the electrons is converted into X-rays, the rate of heat production is approximately equal to the total electrical power supplied to the tube.

The electrical power is given by $P = VI$.

Substituting the given values, $P = 200 \text{ W}$ and $V = 20 \text{ kV} = 20000 \text{ V}$:

$I = \dfrac{P}{V}$

$I = \dfrac{200}{20000} = 0.01 \text{ A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Relative to the energy of the atom with no vacancy, the energy of a silver atom with a vacancy in the $K$ shell is $25.31 \text{ keV}$ higher, in the $L$ shell is $3.56 \text{ keV}$ higher, and in the $M$ shell is $0.530 \text{ keV}$ higher. Determine the frequency of the $K_\alpha$, $K_\beta$, and $L_\alpha$ X-rays of silver.
questions.solution:
The energy of the atom with a vacancy in the $K$, $L$, and $M$ shells are given as $E_K = 25.31 ext{ keV}$, $E_L = 3.56 ext{ keV}$, and $E_M = 0.530 ext{ keV}$.

The energy of the emitted X-ray photon is the difference between the energies of the atom with vacancies in the respective shells.

For the $K_\alpha$ X-ray, the transition is from the $L$ shell to the $K$ shell (the atom goes from a $K$-vacancy to an $L$-vacancy):
$E_{K_\alpha} = E_K - E_L = 25.31 - 3.56 = 21.75 ext{ keV}$

For the $K_ eta$ X-ray, the transition is from the $M$ shell to the $K$ shell (the atom goes from a $K$-vacancy to an $M$-vacancy):
$E_{K_ eta} = E_K - E_M = 25.31 - 0.530 = 24.78 ext{ keV}$

For the $L_\alpha$ X-ray, the transition is from the $M$ shell to the $L$ shell (the atom goes from an $L$-vacancy to an $M$-vacancy):
$E_{L_\alpha} = E_L - E_M = 3.56 - 0.530 = 3.03 ext{ keV}$

Using the relation $ u = \dfrac{E}{h}$, where $h = 6.63 imes 10^{-34} ext{ J s}$ and $1 ext{ eV} = 1.6 imes 10^{-19} ext{ J}$:

$ u_{K_\alpha} = \dfrac{21.75 imes 10^3 imes 1.6 imes 10^{-19}}{6.63 imes 10^{-34}} \approx 5.25 imes 10^{18} ext{ Hz}$

$ u_{K_ eta} = \dfrac{24.78 imes 10^3 imes 1.6 imes 10^{-19}}{6.63 imes 10^{-34}} \approx 5.98 imes 10^{18} ext{ Hz}$

$ u_{L_\alpha} = \dfrac{3.03 imes 10^3 imes 1.6 imes 10^{-19}}{6.63 imes 10^{-34}} \approx 7.32 imes 10^{17} ext{ Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Operating at $40 \text{ kV}$, an X-ray tube has an electric current of $10 \text{ mA}$ flowing from the target to the filament. Assuming that, on average, $1\%$ of the total kinetic energy of the electrons striking the target is converted into X-rays, determine the total power emitted as X-rays.
questions.solution:
The total electrical power supplied to the X-ray tube is given by $P_{in} = VI$.

Substituting the given values:

$P_{in} = 40 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^{-3} = 400 \text{ W}$

This is the total kinetic energy of the electrons striking the target per second.

Since $1\%$ of this energy is converted into X-rays, the total power emitted as X-rays is:

$P_{X} = \dfrac{1}{100} \times P_{in}$

$P_{X} = \dfrac{1}{100} \times 400 = 4 \text{ W}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A piece of tissue paper soaked in polluted water is irradiated with continuous X-rays. The incident X-rays knock electrons out of the inner shells of the sample's atoms, causing excitation. Subsequently, characteristic X-rays are emitted. These emitted X-rays are then analysed, and their intensity is plotted as a function of wavelength (in the figure). Given that only $K_\alpha$ intensities are detected, identify the elements contained in the sample based on the plot. Use Moseley's equation $\nu = (25 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz})(Z - 1)^2$.
questions.solution:
Using Moseley's equation given in the problem:
$ u = (25 imes 10^{14} ext{ Hz})(Z - 1)^2$

We know the relation between frequency $ u$ and wavelength $\lambda$ is $ u = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$, where $c = 3 imes 10^8 ext{ m/s}$.

Substituting $ u$ into the equation and converting $\lambda$ from pm to meters ($\lambda imes 10^{-12} ext{ m}$):
$\dfrac{3 imes 10^8}{\lambda imes 10^{-12}} = 25 imes 10^{14} (Z - 1)^2$

Rearranging to solve for $(Z - 1)^2$:
$(Z - 1)^2 = \dfrac{3 imes 10^{20}}{25 imes 10^{14} imes \lambda} = \dfrac{120000}{\lambda}$

$Z = 1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{120000}{\lambda}}$

Now, we calculate the atomic number $Z$ for each peak wavelength from the given graph:

For $\lambda = 78.9 ext{ pm}$:
$Z = 1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{120000}{78.9}} \approx 1 + 39.0 = 40 \Rightarrow ext{Zr (Zirconium)}$

For $\lambda = 146 ext{ pm}$:
$Z = 1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{120000}{146}} \approx 1 + 28.67 \approx 30 \Rightarrow ext{Zn (Zinc)}$

For $\lambda = 158 ext{ pm}$:
$Z = 1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{120000}{158}} \approx 1 + 27.56 \approx 29 \Rightarrow ext{Cu (Copper)}$

For $\lambda = 198 ext{ pm}$:
$Z = 1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{120000}{198}} \approx 1 + 24.62 \approx 26 \Rightarrow ext{Fe (Iron)}$

The elements present in the sample are Zr, Zn, Cu, and Fe.

Answer: Zr, Zn, Cu, Fe

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A free iron atom emits $K_\alpha$ X-rays having an energy of $6.4 \text{ keV}$. Determine the recoil kinetic energy of the atom. The mass of an iron atom is $9.3 \times 10^{-26} \text{ kg}$.
questions.solution:
By conservation of momentum, the recoil momentum of the atom is equal to the momentum of the emitted X-ray photon.

$p = \dfrac{E}{c}$

The recoil kinetic energy of the atom is given by:

$K = \dfrac{p^2}{2m} = \dfrac{E^2}{2mc^2}$

Given:

$E = 6.4 \text{ keV} = 6.4 \times 10^3 \text{ eV}$

$m = 9.3 \times 10^{-26} \text{ kg}$

$c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$

First, we find the rest mass energy of the iron atom in eV:

$mc^2 = \dfrac{9.3 \times 10^{-26} \times (3 \times 10^8)^2}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \text{ eV} = 52.3125 \times 10^9 \text{ eV}$

Substituting the values into the kinetic energy formula:

$K = \dfrac{(6.4 \times 10^3)^2}{2 \times 52.3125 \times 10^9} \text{ eV}$

$K = \dfrac{40.96 \times 10^6}{104.625 \times 10^9} \text{ eV}$

$K \approx 3.9 \times 10^{-4} \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a photoelectric experiment, the stopping potential exhibits a linear relationship with the inverse of the wavelength $(1/\lambda)$ of the light incident on the cathode. Additionally, the potential difference applied across an X-ray tube is linearly related to the inverse of the cutoff wavelength $(1/\lambda)$ of the emitted X-rays. Determine the value of the common slope of the lines in these two scenarios.
questions.solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation, the stopping potential $V_0$ is given by:

$eV_0 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

$V_0 = \dfrac{hc}{e} \left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\right) - \dfrac{\Phi}{e}$

The slope of the graph of $V_0$ versus $1/\lambda$ is $\dfrac{hc}{e}$.

For an X-ray tube, the cutoff wavelength $\lambda_c$ is given by:

$eV = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_c}$

$V = \dfrac{hc}{e} \left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda_c}\right)$

The slope of the graph of $V$ versus $1/\lambda_c$ is also $\dfrac{hc}{e}$.

The common slope is $\dfrac{hc}{e}$.

Substituting the standard values $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$, and $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$:

$\text{Slope} = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$

$\text{Slope} = 12.42 \times 10^{-7} \text{ V m} = 1.242 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider a monochromatic X-ray beam with a wavelength of $100 \text{ pm}$ passing through a Young's double slit. The resulting interference pattern is recorded on a photographic plate located $40 \text{ cm}$ from the slit. Determine the required separation between the slits if the successive maxima on the screen are to be separated by a distance of $0.1 \text{ mm}$.
questions.solution:
The fringe width in Young's double slit experiment is given by $\beta = \dfrac{\lambda D}{d}$

where $\lambda$ is the wavelength, $D$ is the distance to the screen, and $d$ is the slit separation.

Given:
$\lambda = 100 \text{ pm} = 100 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} = 10^{-10} \text{ m}$
$D = 40 \text{ cm} = 0.4 \text{ m}$
$\beta = 0.1 \text{ mm} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}$

Rearranging the formula to solve for $d$:
$d = \dfrac{\lambda D}{\beta}$

Substituting the values:
$d = \dfrac{10^{-10} \times 0.4}{10^{-4}}$

$d = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}$
